description:
You are acting as an automation-workflow specialist who has just received a raw employee CSV dump.  
Your goal is to clean the raw file, extract one focused report, and compute a simple statistical
summary – all by chaining together nothing more than a few sed and awk one-liners (no external
programming languages, no spreadsheet utilities).  

System layout that already exists when the session starts
---------------------------------------------------------
1. The unaltered dump is stored at  
   /home/user/work/raw/employee_records.csv

2. The file’s exact current contents (including the leading “#” on the first line and the trailing
   blank line) are:

   #id,name,department,salary,join_date
   101,Alice Johnson,Engineering,95000,2015-03-12
   102,Bob Smith,Sales,70000,2016-07-19
   103,Charlie King,Engineering,105000,2014-11-02
   104,Denise Wu,HR,65000,2018-04-23
   105,Evan Li,Engineering,99000,2017-09-14
   106,Farah Ali,Marketing,72000,2019-12-01
   107,George Brown,Engineering,88000,2020-05-30
   108,Helen Clark,Sales,73000,2013-01-25

   (There is one blank line after the final record.)

The tasks you must complete
---------------------------
A. Cleaning pass (use sed)  
   1. Create directory /home/user/work/processed if it does not already exist.  
   2. Generate /home/user/work/processed/employee_records_cleaned.csv with the following rules:  
      • Delete all completely blank lines.  
      • Remove the single leading “#” character from the header line, leaving the header otherwise
        untouched.  
      • Leave every subsequent data line exactly as-is (field order, commas, embedded spaces, etc.).  
   3. The cleaned file must therefore start with  
        id,name,department,salary,join_date  
      followed by the eight data records, one per line, and MUST end with a trailing newline.

B. Focused Engineering salary list (use awk)  
   1. Create directory /home/user/work/reports if it does not already exist.  
   2. Read the cleaned CSV you created and write  
      /home/user/work/reports/engineering_salaries.txt whose content format is:  
        • Header line literally: id|name|salary  
        • One line per employee whose department field is exactly “Engineering”.  
        • Use the pipe “|” as a separator (not a comma).  
        • Sort the lines in strictly descending numerical salary order.  
        • Do NOT pad, quote or truncate fields.  
        • Finish the file with a trailing newline.

C. Department average salary summary (use awk and, if you like, sed to polish)  
   1. Build /home/user/work/reports/dept_avg_salary.log.  
   2. For every distinct department in the cleaned CSV calculate the arithmetic mean of the salary
      column (integer division is fine; no decimals are required).  
   3. Output format must be exactly one
      Department=AverageSalary pair per line, ordered alphabetically by department name.  
   4. Finish the file with a trailing newline.

Verification expectations
-------------------------
The automated checker will commit the following tests:

1. /home/user/work/processed/employee_records_cleaned.csv exists, is non-empty, and its first line
   is the exact string “id,name,department,salary,join_date”.  
2. The cleaned CSV contains 8 data lines and no blanks.  
3. /home/user/work/reports/engineering_salaries.txt exists and matches, byte-for-byte, the precise
   target record set (see internal answer key).  
4. /home/user/work/reports/dept_avg_salary.log exists and contains four lines with the exact
   alphabetical order and format detailed above.

Important constraints
---------------------
• You may touch only the three files called for; do not rename, move or overwrite the
  original raw CSV.  
• Achieve everything with at most 3–4 separate terminal commands total (you do not have to merge
  them into one monster pipeline).  
• Use nothing except the standard GNU core utilities – chiefly sed and awk.  
• Do not embed any hard-coded absolute salary numbers; derive anything you need from the data that
  is provided inside the CSV itself.

Once your commands have run successfully, you’re done. No additional commentary or screen output
is required, although normal command-line output is fine.
truth:
Expected files, their paths and their exact byte-for-byte contents (each line ends with “\n”):

1. /home/user/work/processed/employee_records_cleaned.csv
id,name,department,salary,join_date
101,Alice Johnson,Engineering,95000,2015-03-12
102,Bob Smith,Sales,70000,2016-07-19
103,Charlie King,Engineering,105000,2014-11-02
104,Denise Wu,HR,65000,2018-04-23
105,Evan Li,Engineering,99000,2017-09-14
106,Farah Ali,Marketing,72000,2019-12-01
107,George Brown,Engineering,88000,2020-05-30
108,Helen Clark,Sales,73000,2013-01-25
\n

(“\n” denotes a single newline character.)

2. /home/user/work/reports/engineering_salaries.txt
id|name|salary
103|Charlie King|105000
105|Evan Li|99000
101|Alice Johnson|95000
107|George Brown|88000
\n

3. /home/user/work/reports/dept_avg_salary.log
Engineering=96750
HR=65000
Marketing=72000
Sales=71500
\n
name: task_000000_1c170e5d
